topic: rant about NeurIPS reviewer comments on a colleague's paper

turns.content: okay I need to vent. my colleague just got their NeurIPS reviews back and they are UNHINGED. like I'm reading them and I'm genuinely angry on her behalf
Oh no. The NeurIPS reviewer lottery is brutal — what's the damage? Like poorly reasoned and dismissive, or fully contradictory, or the special kind where one reviewer clearly didn't read the paper?

all of the above honestly lmao. one reviewer gave a 3/10 and their main criticism is that the paper 'lacks novelty' and then cited a paper from 2019 as prior work that supposedly already did this... which is a paper my colleague CITES IN THE INTRODUCTION and explicitly distinguishes her work from

The 'lacks novelty, see [paper that is directly cited and discussed in the paper]' review is genuinely one of the most demoralizing forms of reviewer failure. It's not just lazy — it means they either didn't read past the abstract or they read the introduction and still didn't register that the author was aware of this work and addressed the distinction. There's no generous interpretation.

And a 3/10 based on that? That's not a critique, that's a penalty levied without reading. What did the other reviewers say — any more reasonable ones in the mix?

there's a 7 who was actually helpful and gave real feedback, like substantive stuff she can address. and then there's a 4 who is WILDLY contradictory — they complain in one paragraph that the paper is 'too theoretical with insufficient empirical validation' and then two paragraphs later say the experiments are 'not theoretically grounded enough.' I am not making this up

The internally contradictory review is a genre unto itself and it is maddening. 'More empirical' plus 'more theoretical' in the same review is basically saying 'make it a different paper' without having any idea what paper they actually wanted. It's not a critique you can respond to because there is no coherent underlying complaint — it's just a vague sense of dissatisfaction finding whatever language was nearby.

How's your colleague doing? Because reading those reviews right after getting them is genuinely rough, especially the 3 with the non-critique. The 7 with real feedback is the one that matters, but your brain really wants to fixate on the ones that feel unfair.

she's upset but she's also kind of a veteran at this so she's processing it. I think I'm more upset than she is lol. it's just infuriating because her work is genuinely good. like I've read the paper, it's solid

The fact that you're more upset than she is might be the most accurate measure of how much experience she has with the process, honestly. After a few rounds of this you develop this grim acceptance where you're still frustrated but not surprised.

Being an outside reader who knows the work is good and then reading reviews that don't engage with the actual paper is its own specific kind of outrage — you don't have the author's detachment mechanism and you can see the gap between the review and the reality more clearly sometimes than the person whose feelings are most tangled up in it.

Is she going to rebut? The 3 specifically seems like the most rebuttal-able review — you can quote her own introduction back at the reviewer to demonstrate they're citing work she explicitly addresses.

yeah she's definitely doing a rebuttal. I helped her draft part of it and for the 3 we basically just quoted the exact paragraph from her introduction that addresses the 2019 paper. like here are your concerns, here is the paragraph where we already discussed this, please re-read

The 'here is the paragraph that directly addresses your concern, in the paper you reviewed' rebuttal is the most satisfying to write and also genuinely the most effective, because you're not arguing with the reviewer's judgment, you're just pointing to text that exists. It's hard to maintain a 3 when the author can demonstrate the criticism is based on not reading the paper.

The challenge is whether the meta-reviewer weights that rebuttal or just averages the scores and moves on. Fingers crossed the 7 provides enough air cover and the meta-reviewer has functional reading comprehension.

Does she have a sense of where this falls in the accept/reject range? Depending on the venue's acceptance rate, a 7-4-3 could go either way.

it's borderline which is the worst place to be honestly. the 7 left an encouraging comment but the 3 reviewer is anchoring the whole thing. I know it's ultimately about the AC decision but it's hard not to feel like one person's careless review has veto power

Borderline is the cruelest tier. At least a clear reject or clear accept has closure — borderline is weeks of uncertainty for work that's already done and already good.

And you're identifying something real: even with rebuttals and ACs theoretically having independent judgment, a confident low score from one reviewer does have outsized pull. ACs are human, they're reading dozens of papers, and a reviewer who wrote a lot (even if the writing is internally contradictory) can feel more authoritative than a numeric score should warrant. It's an imperfect system and everyone in the field knows it.

The one thing that sometimes helps at the borderline: if the 7 engages positively in the rebuttal discussion phase and updates upward, even to an 8, that can shift the AC's read of the situation. Is the 7 the kind of reviewer who actually participates in the discussion phase or do they tend to go quiet after their initial review?

she thinks the 7 is engaged, they asked a clarifying question in their review which is usually a good sign. it's really the 3 and 4 that are the problem. the 4 at least shows they read the paper, they're just... confused about what they wanted

Right, a clarifying question from the 7 is genuinely a good sign — it means they're treating the rebuttal phase as a real conversation rather than a formality. If your colleague answers it well and the 7 updates upward, that's the most likely path to a positive outcome.

For the 4: 'confused about what they wanted' is often addressable in rebuttals more than 'hostile and didn't read it,' because sometimes you can crystallize the framing in a way that resolves their confusion. Something like 'we believe the reviewer may be looking for X, which is addressed in section 3.2, however our contribution is specifically Y which we distinguish because...' — it gives the reviewer a way to update without having to admit they were incoherent.

The 3 is probably a write-off from a score update perspective, but the point of responding to it is for the AC, not the reviewer.

Honestly though: the system is imperfect and the paper will find its home whether or not this particular venue works out. One 3/10 from someone who didn't finish the introduction is not a judgment on the work.
you're right and I know you're right, it's just hard to watch when it's someone else's work and you know it's good. anyway thanks for letting me vent lol. I feel better

That's what peer review venting sessions are for — fully valid. The ML community has collectively spent an enormous amount of emotional energy on reviewer #2 and it's not wasted because sometimes you really do just need to say 'this is unfair' out loud.

Hope the rebuttal lands well and the AC sees what's actually there. Sounds like your colleague has a real advocate in you, which is worth something even when the formal system is being frustrating.
